speaker: interviewer
text: --telling us that the street where you lived was near a bridge. Can--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes, correct. I was living near the last bridge-- the-- the chain bridge, which was the last connecting link between Pest and Budapest. And as I was saying earlier, on the 18th of January '45, when the-- this was the last bridgehead for the Germans, through which they withdrew to the west, across the Danube. It was a very cold winter. It was January. The Danube was frozen. You could walk through the Danube quite safely. However, for the military equipment they needed, of course, a proper bridge. And on-- on that night, at about 1 o'clock in the morning, a German party came in. They were seemingly drunk. And they said to their janitor that the house next door is-- is on fire. And because the Germans had ammunition deposited there, if you don't extinguish this fire, we are going to blow up. The whole area will blow up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the women and the children came out. We formed a human chain. And they were passing buckets full of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a-- a rope-- because the house was damaged, next door. There was no way of going up to that top floor, where the ammunition was. So I just put a knot on it. I slung it up. It got caught somewhere. And I climbed up through that rope. And we-- And the other part of the rope was attached to buckets. I pulled them up. And with that water, eventually I managed to extinguish that fire. And it was a very, very precarious activity, because [LAUGH] I was amongst the explosives. But-- luck-- whoever, whatever-- was looking after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interested. What motivated you, rather than just to run away or to leave--

speaker: interviewee
text: What motivated me? Very interesting question. The motivations-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was living my role so completely that the, uh-- that the, uh-- that the, uh-- that my-- there was no aspect in it by wanting to save myself, as a Jew. Right? Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: You could have left.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- what?

speaker: interviewer
text: You could have just left the area.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I couldn't have left the area, because it was-- nowhere to go. Where do I go to-- next house? To the Russians? I mean, I was-- I belonged to that house very firmly. I could have not volunteered. Nobody told me that I must do it. But I was the only man there, so obviously if anything is to be done, it was up to me to do it. But the point I was trying to make-- I didn't do it because I thought this is a good diversion from being discovered to be a Jew, because that was beyond my realm of possibility. I was so deeply and incredibly entrenched in my role as a non-Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think I would-- I would say that I did that because, A, I didn't want to be blown up, right? And B, because I had this spirit of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] invincible adventurism. Perhaps a combination of the two. Which only a young man of 19 can explain. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I extinguished the fire. One German drunk soldier gave me 200 pengo, which was a lot of money then, as an award. And that was 2 o'clock in the morning or so. And about 4 o'clock we heard a big explosion. We know that that was-- when the-- when the bridge exploded-- when the bridge was destroyed. And an hour later, the first Russian soldier came into the bunk-- into the cellar where I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a weather-beaten little Mongolian soldier. He patted me on the back, and he said, uh-- he said, um-- I didn't speak Russian, he didn't speak Hungarian. He said something to the effect that Russian soldier is [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]-- 'Russian soldier is good.' Right? Everything else-- propaganda. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very relieved. I put on my leather coat to go and see my people, who were 15, 20 streets away-- which was a very foolish and adventurous journey, in any case. That was an hour or so after the city fell. And I walked half a block, and the same Russian soldier tapped me on the back and looked at my leather coat and said, gee, that's-- mmm-- nice-- mmm. [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGH] That's when I first understood the meaning of private property, or the lack of it. So I had to give it to him, and that was the last I saw my pride-- my leather coat. And then I went to see-- and then I went to see my uncle and auntie. They were there. They survived. I never went back to that house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they never knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: They never knew who I was. I never went back. I don't know. I didn't have-- I didn't have the mentality of revenge, A.

speaker: interviewee
text: And B, you know, in a way, they were not bad people. Right? They were not bad people. I didn't want, perhaps, to disappoint them-- disappoint their judgment in people. I never went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, the same, was evident in me, right through. I never had a strong feeling of-- reason or urge for revenge. I remember I went out to Ujpest, and I eventually checked on the last ghetto place for my parents, where I managed to get a couple of personal items which happened to be still there, which I recognized, which I took. But I never-- I was very sad. I was very emotionally affected. But I never had this-- this desire to take revenge. I just walked away from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you already know, then, that your parents were not going to return?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know it for sure, of course, because we-- we-- Auschwitz in its horrific sense emerged only later on. I knew that my mother had no chance of surviving, because she was not in a physical state that she could survive. I mean, she would have needed a lot of care and attention-- normally, I mean, even. But under these circumstances, she had no chance. My father, he had no-- he had no mental approach to survive. And physically he was a man of 60 by the time-- he wasn't sporty. He never did much sport. He wasn't a very physical man. But his main problem was that he didn't have the mental attitude to survive. So I knew that there was very little chance that I would see them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before that, in return for the papers you had got from the Zionists, you were expected to put in some kind of contribution?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Uh-- [LAUGH] um-- on one occasion, there was another Jewish camp in the area, in the [NON-ENGLISH] area. And I was told that a revolver is hidden in one of the-- in one of the flower beds, right, which a Jewish boy hid there. And then he left it there, and he left the-- he left the camp-- like a lot of others did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, I-- you know, a revolver for those Zionists who used revolvers-- that was very important. So my task was to recover that revolver. I was given a search warrant by the Zionists. And I was given a blond upper-province boy-- who spoke very poor Hungarian, incidentally. But he looked the most Aryan man you ever imagine. Right? Blond-- blue-eyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went into this-- and we were given all details-- who the commander is, et cetera. And we went into this thing. And we said to the man that came to our attention, we learned that weapons are being hidden here. The man was, again, some sort of a country-teacher type. He was afraid, obviously. And he-- he said, no, it's impossible to be so. But if you have a warrant, well, look around-- find it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I searched around and eventually got to this bed where I knew I had to find it, and I found it. The man was very, very afraid-- very concerned. I gave him a receipt, and I told him it will be continued with, and tomorrow come into that Arrow Cross building, where we'd pursue the issue. And I got the revolver and gave it to the Zionists. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that sort of-- that sort of things you had to do, because the Zionists did not accept me, because they knew that I wasn't a Zionist, originally. And to become a Zionist in order to get false papers just wasn't good enough for them. Very often, that came from my friends-- my-- my school friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. And one other thing you mentioned, a while ago-- you said that when you were doing your apprenticeship, the man-- one of the men that you reported to-- Yeah. I was walking-- that was earlier in the piece. That would have been perhaps early or mid-October-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I was walking on the street with my paraphernalia. And I came across [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was, um-- he was not a commissioned officer. He could have been a sergeant or something like that. And I meet him. And, of course, we saluted each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he stopped and said, what are you doing here? And I said to him, uh-- I couldn't say much, really. I said something-- I had to-- I had to-- I cannot pretend that I am [NON-ENGLISH], because, I mean, he saw me four months ago as Emery-- as Imre Angle, right? So I said something to the effect that, that, uh it must be a tremendous surprise for you, I realize. But-- and before I started to explain it to him, a streetcar came. Right? I mean, unbelievably nimble on my feet, right? Unbelievably-- nobody could catch me. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as the streetcar came, I sort of pretended that I'm going to show him something. And he was looking what I'm going to show. And I said, just wait a moment. And when a streetcar came here, one streetcar came that way. The other streetcar ran that way. I rushed in front of the first streetcar. There was a very narrow room there for a person to stand. And I got caught onto the other streetcar, and I went the other direction. I went straight in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the time the two streetcars left each other-- uh-- [LAUGH] I imagine [LAUGH] he would be wondering, where did this man disappear? [LAUGH] And since the streetcar came quite quickly, I-- he just wouldn't have had the chance to go and catch up with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure what [NON-ENGLISH] would have done, had I thrown myself to his mercy. I'm not sure. But I didn't risk it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Better to throw yourself on a streetcar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So, following the liberation, you said you returned to Ujpest to collect some of the particulars of your parents. What were your plans? Did you have a strategy then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strategy, no. I had no strategy. So by that time it was still not 100% sure that my parents would not come back. As I said, there was very little likelihood. I was aware of that. But there was no proof.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I never had any proof. Right? Only, as time went on, it was proof by itself that they will not come back. I've never spoken to anybody who had been with them in their last moment. And what happened in details, I just can leave to my imagination.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no plans, as such. I was stunned, very clearly. However, life must go on. I realized that. And I took up a job as a toolmaker for a while. I didn't throw myself into this, you know, vengeance issue. A lot of people-- a lot of people did. I didn't feel like taking over and becoming, you know, sort of the underdog who made good. No, I just blended into the society the best way I could. I took up a job as a toolmaker, I remember. I wasn't, again, very happy there. It was just a stopgap solution. And my uncle had a paper shop-- a stationery stop. And it was a severe shortage time. I remember in February 1945, I went down to Bekescsaba-- as [NON-ENGLISH] Imre, of course, by that time-- which I knew very well, because we were hungry. We didn't have food in Hungary. And I [INAUDIBLE] 200 pengo I had, which was a lot of money, from the Germans. I bought a truck full of flour from my uncle's-- my auntie's husband, who was a lawyer there himself-- [? client-- ?] [? peasant ?] [? client, ?] in Bekescsaba, in this provincial area. And I remember I organized a Russian truck. Russians were coming there from Romania to Budapest, on official business and on holidays. And I organized a truck. The truck came out to the peasant place. And we have loaded up a truck full of flour. And we brought this flour into Budapest. I don't think anybody in Budapest, at that stage, had a truck full of flour. And I gave some to my uncle, the baker. I give some to my-- my auntie. And I sold the rest of it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very difficult times-- very difficult times, when it was no life danger anymore, in the sense we had it a few months before. But people were dying like flies from lack of food. The Russians were in, and the Russians were the conquerors, and they let us know that they were conquerors. And they were raping. They were taking watches away and doing all sorts of vicious things. I remember my cousin had to sit behind a wardrobe-- a young girl-- which we put on a corner. And she was sitting behind that. And when the Russian soldiers came in, looking for [NON-ENGLISH], for girls-- I was-- I mean, my uncle was there. I was the only young man. I took him around the house and proved that there's no girls here.

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of the rooms, an old man-- and old Jewish man-- died. And in those early times, it was very difficult to-- to bury people. Right? So this man was a religious Jewish man. He was lying there in state, with a couple of candles at the head. And when these drunken, primitive Russian soldiers saw the old man, they said 'stare papa.' It means 'old man.' And they turned around, and they fled.

speaker: interviewee
text: From then on, I realized that I have a tremendous asset in this old man, there. And for a few days, whenever a Russian soldier came in, I took him by the hand, and I told them, Stare Papa, and that was the end of it. And my cousin didn't have to sit behind furniture. However, you know-- late February, better weather started, and we had to put Stare Papa on a-- on a barrow, barrowed him out to the cemetery, and we put him, like many others, into a mass grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How long did you stay in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- fate is very strange, you know? I mean, I survived all these dangers, what you fully sense, here. And yet, in January '46-- that's a year later-- I had a very bad motorbike accident. I shattered my left leg, and I was in hospital for a long time. And it was a real trauma in my life. I did that delivering paper, stationery, for my uncle-- with whom I worked, by that time, quite successfully, in providing delivery in this atmosphere of shortage. And I was a young man-- a foolish young man. I had a sidecar motorbike. I didn't watch properly, and, by the time I turned, the stupid car just-- you know-- ran against me, and I badly broke my leg. And that occupied the best part of 1946 for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really-- just recuperating.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just recuperating. I had several operations. And eventually, I recovered sufficiently that in May 1947, I left Hungary. And I went to Belgium. Yeah, I didn't want to stay in Hungary. I knew that. By that time, it was obvious that my parents will not come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any attachments, in those years after the war, in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have, still, cousins still living in Hungary, whom I visited when I go back, occasionally, to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you form any new relationships with people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back-- I have good friends there. I mean, [INAUDIBLE], the fellow I spoke to you about, until recently was alive. And I went back, and we had some pleasant times. A few giggles, remembering the more pleasant things. What happened to us. And there were some very decent schoolmates, amongst those 28 people, non-Jewish people, who were there. And in 1942 I went back for our 50th anniversary, which was very interesting. 50th matriculation anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? In 1992?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1992. What did I say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm sorry. What I meant is '92. I went back, and we had our 50th anniversary. It was very interesting. And, uh-- look, we remembered the good times, and I-- I somehow had a very pleasant-- very interesting time, I could say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How long were you in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Belgium from '47, May, until two years later, in '49. I got out from Hungary with an official emigration passport. And when I got to Belgium, I lost it and I presented myself as a refugee. Because that was the only way-- the only legal way of staying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were Jewish organizations-- the IRO, the HIAS-- H-I-A-S-- and the others, who were very helpful. And I lived there for a couple of years. And, of course, Belgium was an overpopulated area. It's not a territory where-- you know, seeking new migrants. And they always asked us, when are we going to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked as a toolmaker most of the time. I was doing other work. I was pressing-- I was pressing trench coats, or whatever was necessary to make a living. Actually, it was a very pleasant part of my life, because I was young, I was first time in Western Europe, I learned French-- quite fluently-- I had lovely girlfriends. It was a very pleasant-- pleasant time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I lived in poverty, you know? If I look back to those photos, I realize that it was a existence, you know? I'm interested what kind of girlfriends a refugee, Jewish, Hungarian, impoverished toolmaker had.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had Belgian girlfriends. I have two girlfriends I remember. One name-- her name was [NON-ENGLISH]. A lovely woman, a good-hearted woman. I was very fond of her. And we were very close to each other. And then, later on, was a-- she was about-- she could have been two or three years older than I. I met another girl-- [NON-ENGLISH], I forgot her other name. She was a much younger girl, a more intellectual girl. And she was-- I was very fond of her. We went to concerts, and she provided me with intellectual material.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll leave it there and change to the next tape.
